channel.title: NYT > Science > Space & Cosmos
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Highlights From the Launch of NASA’s Artemis II Moon Mission
item.description: A giant rocket’s tower of flame lifted three Americans and one Canadian at 6:35 p.m. Eastern on the first crewed journey that will go around the moon since 1972.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: 58 Years After ‘Earthrise,’ NASA’s New Moonshot May Rediscover Earth
item.description: NASA is set to launch the Artemis II mission, sending four astronauts on a trip around the moon. The first time they did this, it changed the world.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Meet the Astronauts of NASA’s Artemis II Moon Mission
item.description: Can the four astronauts of the NASA mission Artemis II make a difference in a distracted and divided world?
item.dc:creator: Timothy Bella and PS Spencer

item.title: Astronaut’s Condition That Led to Space Station Evacuation Remains a Mystery
item.description: The astronaut, Michael Fincke, experienced a medical emergency in January that rendered him unable to speak, he said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:description: Mike Fincke, a NASA astronaut, said doctors aren’t sure what caused his medical episode, which prompted an evacuation from the International Space Station.

item.title: Did Scientists Just Detect an Exploding Black Hole?
item.description: An underwater observatory recently detected a startlingly energetic cosmic neutrino. One possible cause involves a phenomenon that so far exists only in theory.
item.dc:creator: Dennis Overbye

item.title: How Do You Measure Snow From Space? First, Climb a Mountain.
item.description: A new satellite could transform how water is studied worldwide. But to help unlock its capabilities, scientists first needed to take critical measurements on a mountaintop.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Nina Riggio
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times

item.title: A Meteor Exploded Over Ohio. Then the Hunt for Meteorites Began.
item.description: After a seven-ton fireball exploded above the Cleveland area, a group of meteorite hunters descended too, in the name of science — and possibly cash.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Dustin Franz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mark Dayton, an avid meteorite chaser, searches for a fragment late on Thursday.

item.title: NASA’s Hubble Telescope Spots Comet K1 Exploding Into Fragments
item.description: In a stroke of luck, astronomers saw the comet C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) break into four or five fragments in November after it passed close to the sun.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:credit: NASA, ESA, Dennis Bodewits (AU); Image Processing: Joseph DePasquale (STScI)

item.title: Spaceflight Started 100 Years Ago in a Massachusetts Cabbage Patch
item.description: Before humanity sent satellites, telescopes, humans and weapons into space, Robert Goddard experimented with the first liquid-fueled rocket on his aunt’s farm.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan O’Callaghan
item.media:description: Dr. Goddard at Clark University in Worcester, Mass., in the 1920s.

item.title: A Night Light in the Sky? Reflect Orbital Wants to Launch a Big Space Mirror.
item.description: The company is seeking F.C.C. approval to test an idea to reflect sunlight to Earth at night, possibly powering solar panels. Critics say it could be bad for people and wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The start-up Reflect Orbital wants to launch mirror-bearing satellites which it says could address one of the biggest weaknesses of solar power: that electric generation stops when the sun goes down.

item.title: Experiment Shows Possibility of Martian Microbes Hitching a Ride to Earth
item.description: Hardy bacteria in a lab survived pressures comparable to an asteroid strike on the red planet, suggesting a hypothetical scenario in which our planet was seeded with life.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: Norway’s Century-Long Watch on the Northern Lights
item.description: The nation’s northern region has led the scientific quest to understand the aurora borealis. This summer, a 10,000-antenna radar is expected to begin the next phase of exploration.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Michal Siarek
item.media:credit: Michał Siarek for The New York Times

item.title: John Noble Wilford, Times Reporter Who Covered the Moon Landing, Dies at 92
item.description: He gave readers a comprehensive and lyrical account of the historic mission in 1969. His science coverage as a Pulitzer-winning journalist and an author took him around the world.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: John Noble Wilford in 1981. Recalling his coverage of the moon landing, he said, “I thought to myself, yes, this is the biggest story I will probably ever write in my career.”

item.title: Where Are NASA’s Artemis II Astronauts Now? Closer to the Moon Than Earth.
item.description: The astronauts said they had lost track of which day it is on Earth on their transit to the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: In a photo taken by a crew member, the moon is seen from a window on the Orion spacecraft Integrity during the Artemis II mission on Friday.

item.title: How Scientifically Accurate Is ‘Project Hail Mary’? Experts Weigh In
item.description: Based on hard science fiction, a genre that prioritizes scientific accuracy, the blockbuster gets a lot right but misses a few things, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: Houston Cheers on Artemis II Moon Mission, Reclaiming Its Place as ‘Space City’
item.description: The Artemis II mission elicited deep feelings for many Americans, particularly in Houston, the home of mission control.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Meridith Kohut
item.media:description: Over a thousand people gathered at Space Center Houston to watch the launch of the Artemis II rocket.

item.title: NASA’s Artemis II Astronauts Took iPhones Into Space
item.description: The astronauts traveling in the Artemis II spacecraft were allowed to take smartphones with them. Sadly, they can’t connect to the internet.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang

item.title: Artemis II Pilot Test Drove the Orion Capsule on the Way to the Moon
item.description: Victor Glover, a former Navy test pilot, carefully maneuvered the Orion capsule in space around a discarded rocket stage. The demonstration is crucial for future moon landing missions.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: NASA Unveils 1st Earth Photos From Artemis II Moon Mission: ‘You Look Beautiful.’
item.description: The pictures were released on the third day of the first mission since 1972 to send people around the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: The Awe of a Moon Launch in an Age of Trump, Turmoil and Tribal Divisions
item.description: The launch of Artemis II captured the tenor of the times in a country that can still do big things but seems forever mired in big problems.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:description: NASA’s Artemis II rocket on the launchpad at Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Fla., on Wednesday.

item.title: Artemis II Moon Mission: See the Launch, Photos and Video
item.description: With the Artemis II launch, NASA reignited its long-dormant ambitions for crewed lunar exploration.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: The NASA rocket for the Artemis II mission lifting off on Wednesday at Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

item.title: China’s Aiming for the Moon, and NASA Is Looking Over Its Shoulder
item.description: The U.S. space agency launched a lunar flyby Wednesday, but Beijing is pursuing its own space program with formidable focus. Here’s what we know about it, in photos and videos.
item.dc:creator: Selam Gebrekidan and Joy Dong

item.title: As NASA Sent Astronauts to the Moon, Some Rejoiced and Others Barely Noticed
item.description: Space enthusiasts watched with awe and joy as a massive rocket roared into space on the first crewed flight to the moon in more than 50 years. Other Americans were detached, or oblivious.
item.dc:creator: Stella Raine Chu, Dan Simmons, Katrina Miller and Ashley Ahn

item.title: When Will NASA’s Artemis II Mission Reach the Moon?
item.description: After orbiting Earth, the Orion capsule will carry the astronauts to the moon and arrive by Monday night.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn and Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Silva-Martinez family, who drove from Houston to attend the launch, watching the Artemis II launch at Kennedy Space Center.

item.title: What China sees as NASA launches Artemis II.
item.dc:creator: Selam Gebrekidan
item.media:description: A miniature model from Chang’e 6, China’s sixth robotic lunar exploration mission at the Beijing Sci-Fi Carnival last week.

item.title: For the Superfans of Spaceflight, Artemis II Can’t Launch Soon Enough
item.description: Future engineers and former space campers hope to infect people around them with enthusiasm for NASA’s latest moon mission.
item.dc:creator: Scott Cacciola
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times

item.title: Meet ‘Rise,’ Artemis II’s Adorable Zero-Gravity Indicator
item.description: Lucas Ye, age 8, designed the stuffed toy that will start floating when the Orion capsule reaches orbit.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: Jeremy Hansen, of the Canadian Space Agency, holds up Rise, the moon mascot and zero-gravity indicator for the Artemis II test flight.

item.title: Who Made the Artemis II Rocket and Space Capsule?
item.description: While SpaceX and Blue Origin get much of the attention among rocket makers these days, traditional aerospace companies, including Boeing and Lockheed Martin, built the vehicles for Artemis II.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang and Marco Hernandez

item.title: Jeremy Hansen, an Artemis II Astronaut, Is the First Canadian on a Crewed Moon Mission
item.description: The Canadian Space Agency astronaut is the one Artemis II crew member who has not traveled to space.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeremy Hansen, an astronaut with the Canadian Space Agency, will be one of two mission specialists on Artemis II and the first Canadian to circle the moon.

item.title: Christina Koch Will Be the First Female Astronaut to Journey Around the Moon
item.description: Ms. Koch previously set a record for longest single spaceflight by a woman, and was a participant in the first all-female spacewalk.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Christina Koch is one of two mission specialists on Artemis II and will be the first woman to venture into deep space.

item.title: How NASA Is Keeping the Astronauts Safe During the Artemis II Mission
item.description: Years of testing, emergency systems and sensors have gone into protecting the astronauts set to head to the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: Victor Glover, the Artemis II Pilot, Will Be the First Black Astronaut to Circle the Moon
item.description: An astronaut since 2013, Mr. Glover was also the first Black astronaut on a long duration International Space Station mission.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Victor Glover, the pilot for the Artemis II mission, joined NASA’s astronaut corps in 2013 and served at the International Space Station for six months in 2021.

item.title: Who Is Reid Wiseman, Commander of the Artemis II Moon Mission?
item.description: As a child, Mr. Wiseman never imagined he could be an astronaut. Now he’s commanding NASA’s first mission to the moon since 1972.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Reid Wiseman, right, will command NASA’s Artemis II mission. He had a decorated career as a naval aviator before becoming an astronaut in 2009.

item.title: How to Watch NASA’s Artemis II Moon Launch Online
item.description: NASA will broadcast the launch of the Artemis II mission as well as holding live conversations with the crew during their time in space.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and Ceylan Yeğinsu
item.media:description: The Artemis II mission is scheduled to launch on Wednesday evening.

item.title: Meet the four astronauts of Artemis II.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch, Victor Glover and Reid Wiseman at Kennedy Space Center in Florida January.

item.title: Artemis II Completes First Day of Its NASA Lunar Mission
item.description: The crew, three Americans and a Canadian, are the first humans to travel to the moon in more than 50 years. They will not land on the surface, but the mission will pave the way for future visits.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: NASA’s Mission Back to the Moon
item.description: Artemis II, which would be the first time anyone would travel this far from Earth since 1972, is set to launch on April 1. Kenneth Chang, a reporter for The New York Times, describes how the mission is key to a U.S. goal.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang, Marco Hernandez, Melanie Bencosme, Jon Miller, Gabriel Blanco, Joey Sendaydiego and Luke Piotrowski

item.title: Why the Artemis II Astronauts Will Be Wearing Orange
item.description: It’s not just any orange. It’s International Orange.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:description: The Artemis II crew, in an orange officially known as AMS Standard 595 color #FS 12197.

item.title: First Canadian Astronaut Will Travel to the Moon Amid Fraying U.S.-Canada Relations
item.description: Canada will send its first astronaut to the moon on a joint mission with the United States, but back on Earth, the relationship between the two countries is fraying.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert Lucas of MDA Space helped develop the Canadarm3, a robotic system that will support astronauts during spacewalks and aid repairs in orbit.

item.title: For Trump, the Artemis II Moon Mission Offers a Shot to Cement His Legacy
item.description: No president since the Apollo era has pushed harder to return to the moon than President Trump. But he wants a space achievement that is about “more than getting rocks this time.”
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:description: President Trump visiting the Kennedy Space Center for a SpaceX launch during his first term, in May 2020.

item.title: NASA Is Launching Astronauts to the Moon, but Americans Aren’t That Excited
item.description: Polling has consistently found that most people would prefer NASA spend money on things like monitoring climate change and averting asteroid collisions rather than human spaceflight.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: Artemis II pilot Victor Glover met with young space enthusiasts at the Kennedy Space Center in January. While overall opinions of NASA remain high, polls find human exploration of the moon ranks pretty low.

item.title: For NASA’s Artemis II Crew, Journey to the Moon ‘Starting to Feel Real’
item.description: The four astronauts — three Americans and one Canadian — spoke from a prelaunch quarantine ahead of their scheduled Wednesday mission.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: From left, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch, Reid Wiseman and Victor Glover, members of the Artemis II crew, after arriving at the Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Fla., on Friday. Mr. Wiseman is holding a plush toy named Rise that will fly in their capsule during their journey to the moon.
